1: A theme: "Why do you boast in evil, O mighty man? The steadfast love of God endures all day." The contrast of human wickedness and enduring divine hesed — the boastful evil of the "mighty man" set against the permanence of covenant love — is the psalm's structural irony. Romans 3:27: "where then is boasting? It is excluded." The boasting in human power that Psalms 52:1 ridicules is the same boasting that Paul says the cross eliminates: 1 Corinthians 1:29 ("so that no one may boast before him"). The "mighty man" who boasts in evil is the antithesis of Christ, who "being in very nature God, did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage" (Philippians 2:6).
2: A theme: "Your tongue plots harm, sharp as a razor blade — you craftsman of deceit." The tongue as the weapon of the wicked — the razor-sharp, craft-worked deceit — is the failure that James 3:6 describes: "the tongue also is a fire, a world of evil among the parts of the body." The tongue of the "Doeg figure" (the informant who betrayed the priests of Nob, 1 Sam 22) is the tongue of all whose words destroy rather than build. Christ, by contrast, is the Word made flesh (John 1:14) — the divine speech whose every word gives life rather than death (John 6:63: "the words I have spoken to you — they are full of the Spirit and life").
3: A theme: "You love evil rather than good, and lies rather than truth." The moral inversion — the preference for evil over good, lies over truth — is what John 8:44 attributes to the devil: "he was a murderer from the beginning, not holding to the truth, for there is no truth in him. When he lies, he speaks his native language, for he is a liar and the father of lies." Those who follow the pattern of Doeg/Psalms 52:3 participate in the devil's nature rather than Christ's: "I am the way and the truth and the life" (John 14:6). The love of lies over truth is the opposite of the one who is himself the truth.
4: A theme: "You love every word that destroys, you treacherous tongue." The destructive tongue — words as weapons rather than life-givers — is the pattern that Proverbs consistently contrasts with wisdom: "the mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence" (Proverbs 10:11). At Jesus's trial, false witnesses loved "words that destroy" (Mark 14:56-59: "many bore false witness against him — but their statements did not agree"). Christ endured the destroying words of false accusation without retaliation, "entrusting himself to him who judges justly" (1 Peter 2:23).
5: A theme: "But God will pull you down permanently; he will grab and rip you from your home; he will tear you out of the land of the living." The eschatological reversal of the wicked's apparent security — torn from the land of the living permanently — is the covenant consequence that the NT presents as eschatological judgment. Matthew 25:41 ("depart from me, you who are cursed, into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels"); Revelation 20:15 ("anyone whose name was not found written in the book of life was thrown into the lake of fire"). The tearing down of the wicked in Psalms 52:5 is the eschatological judgment that Christ will execute as the enthroned judge (Acts 17:31).
6: A theme: "The righteous will see and be filled with awe, and they will laugh." The awe and laughter of the righteous at divine judgment — the vindication that the suffering righteous experience when divine justice is enacted — is the pattern of the eschatological reversal. Revelation 19:1-4 describes the heavenly multitude's worship at the fall of Babylon ("Hallelujah! The Lord our God, the Almighty, reigns"). Luke 6:21 (Jesus: "blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh") promises the laughter of eschatological vindication to those who mourn under the wicked's power. The righteous who fill with awe in Psalms 52:6 are those whose faith has persisted through persecution.
7: A theme: "'Here is the man who would not take refuge in God, but relied on his great wealth and found his strength in destruction.'" The epitaph of the wicked — the negative inscription over a life built on the wrong foundation — is the warning of Jesus's parable of the two foundations (Matthew 7:24-27). The man who builds on sand ("wealth and destruction") instead of the rock (Christ's words) comes to ruin when the storm comes. The "refuge in God" that the wicked of Ps 52 spurned is the refuge in Christ that the NT consistently identifies as the only stable foundation: "whoever trusts in him will never be put to shame" (Romans 10:11).
8: A theme: "But I am like a flourishing olive tree in the house of God; I trust in God's steadfast love forever and ever." The olive tree as the image of the righteous person rooted in YHWH's house — flourishing, fruitful, enduring — is the OT's horticultural image of covenant blessing (Psalms 1:3; Jeremiah 11:16). John 15:1-5: "I am the true vine, and my Father is the gardener... you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit." Christ is the true vine/tree in whom the believer is rooted; the flourishing olive tree of Psalms 52:8 is the image fulfilled in those who abide in Christ, drawing life from his life.
9: A theme: "I will give you thanks forever, because of what you have done; I will hope in your name, for it is good, before your faithful people." The eternal thanksgiving grounded in divine action — "because of what you have done" — is the posture of the redeemed community. Revelation 11:17: "we give thanks to you, Lord God Almighty, the One who is and who was, because you have taken your great power and have begun to reign." The hope in the divine "name" that is "good" is the hope in the name of Jesus that is "above every name" (Philippians 2:9-10) — the Name that is the ground of the eternal thanksgiving that Psalms 52:9 commits to.
